The memory math is immediate: Scale those numbers up and you get the modern deployment story: int4 is the reason multi-billion-parameter models run on laptops and phones, and the reason 13.6's guest star fits in this tab at all. The quality cost is real but smaller than intuition suggests.

title: 13.4 Quantization
text: Naive rounding hurts; the good methods — small per-group scales, error-compensating rounding, activation-aware choices about which weights deserve precision (the GPTQ/AWQ family) — keep 4-bit surprisingly close to full quality, with degradation showing up first in long-tail precision: rare words, delicate phrasing. The lab makes it a reading exercise: the same prompt at fp16, int8, and int4, side by side. Expect int8 to be imperceptible and int4 to occasionally flatten a phrase — a cost you can now see rather than take on faith. The time machine's scrubbable checkpoints ship as int4/int8 — that's what makes carrying a dozen snapshots per bird affordable. But the designated resume points ship fp16, because training on top of quantized weights with fresh optimizer state is not something that works, and we don't pretend otherwise. Inference forgives rounding; optimization doesn't.

title: 13.5 Speculative Decoding
text: Little live-Quill drafts a burst of tokens; big flagship-Quill checks the whole burst in one pass and keeps what it agrees with. Same output distribution, several times the speed — your two birds, cooperating at last. The trick rides on an asymmetry: generating is sequential, checking is parallel. A big model's per-token latency is dominated by reading its weights from memory — and one forward pass over five tokens reads the weights once, same as a pass over one. Verifying a burst costs about the same as generating a single token. So let something cheap write the burst. The protocol: the draft model (live-Quill, a few million parameters, fast) proposes, say, 5 tokens. The target model (flagship-Quill, 25M) runs one batched forward pass over all 5, which yields its own next-token distribution at every position. Compare, position by position: accept while the target agrees, stop at the first rejection, resample that one position from a corrected distribution, and hand the new tail back to the draft. The startling part is the guarantee.

title: 13.5 Speculative Decoding
text: The acceptance rule — take a drafted token with probability , and on rejection sample from the renormalized excess — makes the output distribution exactly what the target alone would have produced. Not approximately: token for token, mathematically identical. Speculative decoding is that rare deal — speed for free, quality untouched — paid for only in extra compute when the draft guesses wrong. So the speedup lives and dies by the acceptance rate. Most tokens of English are easy — "the", punctuation, the obvious next word — and a small model drafts them just as well as a big one, which is why acceptance runs high and the wins are real. When the models disagree often (say, after heavy RL reshapes one of them), the bursts shrink and the overhead wins. One hard requirement: both models must share a tokenizer — and yours do, by construction. The pocket-and-flagship pair already sitting in your browser's memory is the natural demo, and it arrives with the flagship milestone.

title: 13.6 The SmolLM Cameo
text: A real pretrained 135M-parameter model runs beside Quill for a direct conversation between scales. Same organism, more scale — the point of the whole course, made talkable. The guest is a SmolLM-class model, 135M parameters, running in this same browser tab via transformers.js — the same download that powers the AI tutor and Part 8's generator, so it costs you nothing new. It arrives with a later milestone; what it teaches can be said now: almost everything you know transfers. Open its config file and you can read every field, because every field is a chapter you took. Byte-level BPE tokenizer — Part 2's algorithm with a bigger vocabulary. Decoder-only blocks with RoPE, pre-norm, a gated MLP — Part 3, stacked deeper and wider. Grouped-query attention — 13.3's cache formula, being fought. The sampling loop and KV cache serving its replies are the ones you just studied. Rough shape of the comparison: What actually changed is the exponents — above all the data: thousands of times more tokens, from carefully curated mixtures. And here's the kicker for anyone who took Part 8: a large share of SmolLM's training diet is synthetic textbooks , generated and filtered at industrial scale.

title: 13.6 The SmolLM Cameo
text: The reveal that TinyStories was synthetic wasn't a toy-course curiosity — it's how real small models are fed now. Put a prompt to Quill and the cameo side by side and you're not comparing two species. You're looking at the same organism at two points on the scaling curve — which is the cleanest possible setup for the epilogue's question: what, exactly, do the exponents buy?

title: Part 14 · Epilogue
text: You raised two birds from random weights to aligned, tool-using models. The epilogue asks the only question left: how much of what you did is what the frontier labs do — and how much changes on the way up? This chapter's interactive playground arrives with a later milestone.

title: 14.1 The Scale Map
text: A guided walk from Quill and Rook to trillion-parameter systems: the surprising list of things that are literally identical — the loss, the block, the recipes — and the honest list of things that only exist at scale, from emergent abilities to entire data centers. Lay the two pipelines side by side and the punchline isn't the size gap — it's the shape match. Same stages, in the same order, for the same reasons. Different exponents.

title: 14.1 The Scale Map
text: Dimension Your birds A frontier lab Parameters 2–5M live pair; 15–25M flagships Hundreds of billions; trillions with MoE Training tokens Millions — a coffee break Tens of trillions Vocabulary ~2k UCI moves; small byte-BPE 100k+ byte-BPE — same algorithm Context window A few hundred tokens Hundreds of thousands and up Hardware One browser tab, one GPU Tens of thousands of accelerators Pretraining wall-clock ~3 minutes Months Data pipeline Curated slices of TinyStories + Lichess Web-scale crawls, petabyte dedup, curation teams Post-training labels Your ~100 clicks + a shipped set Thousands of annotator-hours, plus RLVR farms Evals Legal-move gauge, judge scores, OOD positions Sprawling benchmark suites, human evals, red teams Cost Free Nine figures and climbing Now the list that should genuinely surprise you — what is literally identical , not analogous: The loss. Cross-entropy on next-token prediction, unchanged since Part 4. The block. Attention, RoPE, pre-norm, gated MLP — Part 3's anatomy, stacked higher. The tokenizer algorithm — Part 2's byte-BPE, with a bigger merge table. The train/val discipline, and the eval anxiety that comes with it (Parts 4–5).

title: 14.1 The Scale Map
text: The post-training arc: SFT, then preferences, then verifiable rewards (Parts 7–11). The serving stack: temperature, top-p, KV cache, quantization, speculation (Part 13). You did not run a simplified sketch of the pipeline. You ran the pipeline, small. What you never touched exists only at scale: capability jumps that arrive unannounced somewhere past a few billion parameters, in-context learning broad enough to feel general, and an entire engineering discipline — splitting one training step across thousands of chips (data, tensor, pipeline, and expert parallelism) — that a single browser tab never needs. Plus the parts that aren't math at all: data acquisition, safety review, eval institutes, teams. Nothing conceptual separates your birds from the frontier — that's the course's thesis, and by now it's your lived experience. What separates them is tokens, chips, and time. That should feel both comforting and sobering: comforting because you actually understand these systems now; sobering because understanding was never the bottleneck.

title: 14.2 What Nobody Knows
text: Why does grokking happen? What do models really represent? Why does the scaling line stay straight? The open problems of the field, stated plainly — along with a sober note on safety, and on what toy models can and cannot teach about it. Why does grokking happen? You watched it in Part 4: a hatchling memorizes its training set, sits at chance on validation for thousands of steps, then abruptly generalizes. There are stories — weight decay slowly favoring simpler circuits over lookup tables, competing sub-networks trading places — but no theory that predicts when a given run will grok, or whether it will at all. A phenomenon you can replay on demand in a browser remains genuinely unexplained. What do probes actually show? Part 6's linear probes read a board off Rook's residual stream, and patching flipped its move by editing its belief about one square. Convincing — and still contested. Does the model use a world model, or a bag of heuristics that happens to be linearly decodable? Where is the line between representing and correlating? Interpretability has sharp tools and no agreed success criterion, at 25M parameters or at a trillion. Does alignment hold under pressure?

title: 14.2 What Nobody Knows
text: Parts 9 through 11 gave you the honest arc: learned rewards get hacked, KL is a leash and not a proof, and verifiable rewards close the gap only where a verifier exists. Most of what we actually want from models — honesty, judgment, care — has no checker. Whether oversight can scale to systems more capable than their overseers is arguably the field's most consequential open problem, and your reward-hacking scars are a miniature of why. Are emergent abilities real? Capabilities seem to appear suddenly at scale — arithmetic, translation, multi-step reasoning. But a sharp counter-argument says some of those jumps are artifacts of the measuring stick: underlying skill improves smoothly, while an all-or-nothing metric (exact-match answers, say) shows nothing until it shows everything. The debate is live, unresolved, and has real stakes: it decides whether the next order of magnitude holds surprises by default. Why are the scaling laws straight? Your Part 5 mini-lab plotted three hatchling sizes and saw the hint of a line.

title: 14.2 What Nobody Knows
text: The full version — loss against compute, straight on log axes across many orders of magnitude — is the empirical bedrock the industry plans nine-figure training runs on. Nobody has a first-principles derivation of why it holds, how long it continues, or what it is really measuring. The field's most load-bearing fact is a mystery with error bars. None of these are homework the field forgot to finish. They are the frontier — and the point of listing them here is that you now know enough to read the papers arguing about every single one.

title: 14.3 Two Birds, Fully Grown
text: A last look at the stat sheets: every stage badge earned, every eval score, every token seen since the moment you scrubbed both birds back to nothing. They're yours now — and so is the understanding of how they came to be. Quill hatched as noise and babbled its way to grammar while you watched the loss fall. It learned to follow instructions, then learned your taste from a hundred clicks, then hacked that taste until the KL leash reined it in, then submitted to rewards that were simply true — five sentences, one lantern. Somewhere along the way it stopped being a curve on a chart and started being a storyteller with habits you recognize, because you put them there. Rook went from random mover to legal player to something stranger: a model that holds a board in its activations, whose belief about one square you once flipped with your own cursor. It hung its king for a queen when you paid it to, and learned better when the reward was checkmate itself. Its Elo is on the stat sheet. Its opening habits are yours to blame. A sober note to carry out the door. Tiny models teach mechanism : you watched Goodhart's law fire, watched a KL term contain it, watched a verifier make reward honest.

title: 14.3 Two Birds, Fully Grown
text: They do not teach alignment at scale . A 25M-parameter storyteller cannot deceive you in ways that matter, pursue goals across contexts, or surprise its overseers — and the failure modes that might do those things may simply not miniaturize. Take the mechanisms with you. Do not take the comfort. The weights are yours — sitting in your browser's storage, scrubbable back to the moment they were noise. So is the recipe: every stage a frontier lab runs, you have run, small. When the next paper drops — a new optimizer, a routing trick, a post-training loop — it will land somewhere on a map you have personally walked. The field is young, its biggest questions are open, and the distance from your two birds to the frontier is exponents, not magic. Go raise something.
